sources.name: Missing -canary2 remote sidecar rides the FULL 30-min wait budget before reporter_unreachable (finding-7 latency half)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/verify_canary.py:106-123 (_classify_poll_failure), :851-962 (poll loop); src/hpc_agent/infra/cluster_status.py:185-199; src/hpc_agent/execution/mapreduce/reduce/status.py:1076-1099 (_emit_err sidecar_not_found, exit 2); root cause: src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_flow.py:1270-1315 (_mirror_canary_sidecar is LOCAL-only) + :2379-2433 (fire_second_canary runs post-deploy, 'no transport' — the -canary2 sidecar never ships)
sources.mechanism: The cluster-side status reporter reads .hpc/runs/<run_id>.json REMOTELY. The first canary's sidecar reaches the cluster only because its mirror happens pre-rsync; fire_second_canary mints the -canary2 sidecar AFTER deploy and nothing pushes it. Every poll then gets the reporter's structured sidecar_not_found error at rc=2 — but _classify_poll_failure only escalates rc 126/127 as deterministic; rc 2 is 'transient', so the loop re-polls until the budget elapses, then returns reporter_unreachable (a false negative that also blocked the fleet boundary).
sources.cost: wait_budget_sec = 1800s (default in both verify_canary.py:89 and SubmitAndVerifySpec _wire/workflows/submit_and_verify.py:69) of pure spin, ~130-170 failed SSH polls (ramp 3,6,10s ceiling; each poll = cold SSH + conda activation + python launch ≈ 3-6s). Live run 13: the -canary2 verify spun this budget while the real -canary job had COMPLETED exit 0 in 97s (_runtime.json; job elapsed 1m39s) — ~30 min of the observed ~hour canary stage is this defect.
sources.trigger: Every double-canary submit (default-on; HPC_NO_DOUBLE_CANARY only opt-out) whose deploy ran before the second sidecar was minted — i.e. every gated S2 today. Same class fires for any deterministic reporter error that is not rc 126/127 (sidecar_parse_error, tasks_py_not_found).
sources.fix: Two layers, both proper: (a) eliminate the failure — one-definition of the canary sidecar name and ship it: fire_second_canary must push the freshly minted sidecar (one scp/ssh write of a <1KB JSON, or mint ALL canary sidecars pre-deploy) and disclose which name it will poll; (b) make the reporter's structured error codes first-class in _classify_poll_failure — sidecar_not_found / sidecar_parse_error / tasks_py_not_found are deterministic (will never heal by waiting) and must take the existing 3-poll escalation path (~20s) instead of the 1800s budget, with the error code named in the verdict.

sources.name: Double canary is fully serial — a second queue-wait + run + verify cycle inserted between 'canary verified' and the S2 brief
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_and_verify.py:226-307 (_run_double_canary), :564-578 (placed after first verify, before the stop_after_canary return); src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_flow.py:2379-2433 (fire_second_canary)
sources.mechanism: The n=2 determinism prior fires the -canary2 job only AFTER the first canary is verified, then runs a second full verify_canary poll loop, then two rsync pulls (_pull_canary_task0_metrics x2) to mint the sample. Nothing in the diff requires ordering — both executions are independent 1-task jobs of the same command; serializing them doubles the canary stage's queue-wait and detect tail on the critical path of every fresh gated submit.
sources.cost: +1x (scheduler queue wait + task runtime + detect tail + verify tail) per submit. Live run: task runtime 97s, job elapsed 1m39s; with a few minutes of queue wait the second cycle adds ~3-10 min wall clock even when nothing is wrong. Arithmetic: total canary stage = 2*(queue+run+detect) instead of max() of two concurrent jobs (~half).
sources.trigger: Every fresh gated submit (submit-s2) with the double canary enabled (the default).
sources.fix: Fire both canary jobs at Phase-1 submit time (two qsubs back-to-back — both sidecars minted and shipped pre-deploy), let them queue/run concurrently, then verify both. The scheduler parallelizes the wait for free; the n=2 diff consumes both outputs regardless of completion order. Verify loops can share one reporter poll (the reporter already accepts job_ids lists) or run sequentially over already-terminal jobs (cheap).

sources.name: Canary TTL cache (#249) never fires on the gated S2 path — every submit-s2 re-pays the full canary stage inside the 4h TTL
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_flow.py:273-289 (canary_only=True -> ALWAYS canary, cache consulted only at :297-313 for the non-gated path); src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_and_verify.py:499-502 (Phase 1 always canary_only=True), :564-576 (comment: 'the skip only bites the non-gated submit_flow path'); cache: src/hpc_agent/state/canary_cache.py:56 (DEFAULT_TTL_SEC=14400)
sources.mechanism: submit_and_verify unconditionally submits Phase 1 with canary_only=True, and _canary_decision short-circuits canary_only before the #249 cache check. Since the block decomposition made submit-s2 the standard path, the cache built to save '30s-30min of wall-clock per submit' (its own module docstring) is structurally dead there: a cmd_sha canary-validated minutes ago still pays canary + double-canary + verify. Meanwhile submit-s3's predecessor gate (_assert_canary_verified, ops/submit_blocks.py:262-296) consumes exactly this cache — so the artifact exists and is trusted, just never used to skip.
sources.cost: Full canary stage per repeat submit within TTL: 2x(queue + 97s run + detect) + verify tails ≈ 5-20 min avoidable per re-submit of an already-validated cmd_sha (e.g. an S2 rerun after a spec nudge that doesn't move cmd_sha, or a retry after a worker crash like run 13's).
sources.trigger: Any submit-s2 for a (cmd_sha, version, cluster, code-sha) key already canary-validated within HPC_CANARY_TTL_SEC (14400s default).
sources.fix: Honor the cache one level up: submit_and_verify checks is_canary_validated_fresh BEFORE Phase 1 and, on a hit, returns verified=True with the cache-hit disclosure line (the same CAUTION text _canary_decision already renders) instead of firing the probe — S3's gate already keys on the identical cache entry so the chain stays consistent. Note: the current bypass is a documented design choice ('the two-phase gate is an explicit request to validate'); this needs a maintainer ruling, but the cache being 100% dead on the standard path looks like drift, not intent.

sources.name: Post-terminal verify tail is 3-6 serial cold-SSH round trips (plus 2 rsync pulls) that the reporter could return in one
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/verify_canary.py:1039-1052 (fetch_task_logs), :1068-1077 (_verify_remote_checkpoint, 1 ssh), :1192-1197 (verify_combiner_artifact, 1 ssh), :1247-1252 (_read_canary_exit_code, 1 ssh via cat _runtime.json), :1280-1295 (fingerprint sha256sum, 1 ssh); src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_and_verify.py:82-152 (_pull_canary_task0_metrics — one rsync_pull PER canary, called twice at :194-195)
sources.mechanism: Once the poll loop breaks terminal, verification issues a chain of independent single-purpose SSH execs: stderr-tail fetch, optional checkpoint probe (a full remote python launch), optional output-existence test, _runtime.json exit-code read, optional sha256 — each its own connection (no ControlMaster on native Windows; the persistent broker was deleted), each paying handshake + auth. The double canary repeats the chain, then two more serial rsync sessions pull the two metrics.json files.
sources.cost: Estimate: 6-12 round trips x ~1.5-4s cold-SSH each = ~10-45s per gated submit (worse under hoffman2's evening MaxStartups throttle, where cold connections were measured ~150x slower than the old broker channel per the 2026-07-06 session). Pure structural overhead — every byte read is already reachable by the reporter process that just ran on the login node.
sources.trigger: Every canary verification (success and failure paths both pay the tail fetch + exit-code read); x2 with the double canary; +2 rsyncs when the sample mints.
sources.fix: Fold the terminal verification into the reporter: when the reporter observes the run terminal, have it emit stderr tail (it already knows the log path), _runtime.json exit_code, expect_output existence, and the optional sha256 in the SAME JSON envelope as the status poll that detects terminal — zero additional round trips. The two sample pulls collapse to one rsync with two include paths (same host, same session).

sources.name: Each detect poll launches conda activation + TWO python interpreters on the login node instead of a cheap scheduler query
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/cluster_status.py:42 (_ENV_PYTHON), :159-170 (import-guard `python -c 'import hpc_agent'` THEN `python -m ...reduce.status` per poll); the cheap alternative exists at :253-296 (ssh_batch_scheduler_states — one qstat/squeue, no python); poll loop: src/hpc_agent/ops/verify_canary.py:883-893
sources.mechanism: Every poll in the wait loop runs the full python status reporter (sidecar read + tasks.py import + results-tree scan) plus a separate guard interpreter launch, under conda activation on an NFS home — when all the loop needs until terminal is 'has the job left running/pending'. The heavyweight reporter is only required once, at the terminal poll, to get completion counts.
sources.cost: Estimate ~2-6s remote exec per poll (conda activate + 2 python startups on NFS + results scan) vs <0.5s for a qstat: for a 97s canary at the 10s ceiling that's ~10-13 heavy execs (~30-70s of remote work, and the last-poll exec time adds directly to detect latency); in the finding-1 spin it was ~150+ heavy execs in 30 min, which is also SSH-throttle exposure on hoffman2. Detect-after-end latency = up to one ceiling interval (10s wire default / 30s CLI default, verify_canary.py:88, _wire/.../submit_and_verify.py:64) + the poll's own exec time. (monitor_flow's HPC_STATUS_POLL_INTERVAL_SEC 10s floor does not apply here — verify_canary deliberately fast-starts at 3s.)
sources.trigger: Every canary wait poll on every gated submit (x2 canaries).
sources.fix: Split liveness from verification: poll scheduler state with ssh_batch_scheduler_states (already built, one qstat/squeue, sentinel-acked) while the job is queued/running, and invoke the full reporter exactly once when the job leaves the queue (plus the finding-4 fused terminal read). Longer-term the §5 cluster-side watcher could push the terminal event (it already piggybacks .hpc_watcher_ALARM on the poll channel), replacing detection polling entirely; the qstat-until-gone split is the proper near fix and drops per-poll cost ~5-10x with zero cadence change.

census.notes: Stage B decomposition of the live run-13 hour: staging dominated (already fixed tonight), but the canary tail contributed ~30+ min: (1) the -canary2 sidecar_not_found spin rides the FULL 1800s wait budget because reporter rc=2 is classified 'transient' (only rc 126/127 escalate at 3 polls) — measured constants: wait_budget_sec=1800 (verify_canary.py:89), fast-poll floor 3s (HPC_CANARY_FAST_POLL_SEC, :238), ceiling = poll_interval_sec (wire default 10s, CLI default 30s). (2) The double canary serializes a second full queue+run(97s)+verify cycle between 'verified' and the S2 brief — the verified→brief gap IS the second canary plus two rsync pulls plus local persist (persist/render itself is local and cheap, ops/submit_blocks.py:105-183). Healthy-path detect for the live 97s job was actually good: ramp polls at ~3,9,19,...,99,109s → detected ~10s after end; the avoidable seconds-to-minutes live in the serial double canary, the dead TTL cache on the gated path, the 6-12 serial cold-SSH verify-tail execs, and above all the deterministic-error-as-transient misclassification. Deliberately excluded as <1s or failure-only-minor: watchdog stamp floor churn (10s floor, local), vanished-grace delay (~poll_interval before completed_unknown on the fast-fail path, ~10-30s but a correctness grace tied to scheduler registration), canary-cache flock (local). Out of scope honored: scheduler queue wait itself; but note finding 2 doubles exposure to it and finding 3 re-pays it entirely. Finding 3 flags a documented design comment ('canary_only → neither optimization applies') — needs a maintainer ruling before treating as pure defect.

sources.name: Pull side has NO delta/cache/batching/checkpointing — a failed or timed-out pull re-pays the entire transfer
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/__init__.py:620-676 (_scp_pull), :1307-1403 (rsync_pull); timeout ceiling at src/hpc_agent/infra/remote.py:88 (RSYNC_TIMEOUT_SEC=1800)
sources.mechanism: On native Windows (the live control plane — rsync absent, _have_rsync() false at transport/__init__.py:1366) every pull falls to _scp_pull: a single monolithic `scp -r` into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() staging dir (line 651). None of the push side's fixes exist here: no content-hash manifest delta (_remote_push_manifest / manifest_delta, lines 856-870 push-only), no .hpc hash cache, no bounded batches (_delta_ship_batches, line 904 push-only), no per-batch checkpoint (_write_push_manifest, line 940 push-only). On any failure — VPN sever, or the RSYNC_TIMEOUT_SEC=1800s ceiling killing a >30-min pull — the `with TemporaryDirectory()` context DISCARDS every byte already transferred, and the retry starts from zero bytes.
sources.cost: MEASURED: run-12's 40-90-min double-pull (docs/design/history run-12 finding 28 context, cited in aggregate_flow.py:396-401 — 'the old behavior paid the full results/ mirror twice'). Arithmetic: a 1.18 GB results mirror (run-13 finding 4's measured payload) over the ~1 MB/s VPN (run-#11 measured rate) = ~20 min per attempt; one timeout/sever doubles it to ~40 min, matching the observed 40-90 min.
sources.trigger: Any aggregate/harvest pull on a Windows control plane that dies mid-transfer or exceeds 1800s: the _combiner pull (aggregate_flow.py:704), the per-task fallback pull (:414), the trace pull (:578), the summaries pull (:1653). Certain on multi-GB per-task fallback pulls over VPN.
sources.fix: Give the pull the push's exact architecture: a `ssh tar c | tar x` PULL analogue (the symmetric inverse of _tar_ssh_push) that (a) computes a content-hash delta against local files already present (reuse infra/manifest.py manifest_delta — the local side can hash itself instantly, the remote side via the same one-round-trip hash snippet _remote_push_manifest already uses), (b) ships in bounded batches landing DIRECTLY in the destination (each landed batch durable), so a retry's delta re-derives landed files and pulls only the remainder. Eliminates re-payment entirely; also retires the TemporaryDirectory discard-on-failure.

sources.name: _scp_pull ignores include filters — every 'filtered' pull transfers the entire subtree on Windows
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/__init__.py:620-645 (docstring admits it: 'The include filter is not honored (scp has no equivalent)'); consumers at ops/aggregate_flow.py:414-420 (include=[summary_name]), :578-584 (include=[_trace.jsonl]), :936-942 (include=[metrics_aggregate.json]), :1653-1658 (include=[summary_glob])
sources.mechanism: rsync_pull's include contract ('only matching patterns are transferred') is silently void on the live Windows path: _scp_pull does `scp -r` of the whole remote_subdir. The per-task fallback believes it is pulling only KB-scale summary JSON sidecars ('filtered to metrics.json files so the transfer stays small', aggregate_flow.py:384-385) but actually mirrors every task's full output tree — CSVs, chunks, logs — under the scoped results prefix. The docstring's 'this is acceptable' claim predates the per-task fallback becoming the default no-combiner path.
sources.cost: MEASURED consequence: the run-12 40+ min results pulls and the 1.18 GB _per_task_results mirror (run-13 finding 4) exist ONLY because the filter is ignored — the actual summary JSONs for a 20-2700-task run are ~KB-MB total (aggregate_flow.py:442 cites 2700 mirrored files where only the metrics.json set was needed). Filter honored: seconds. Filter ignored: 20-90 min.
sources.trigger: Every filtered pull from a Windows control plane where the run's per-task dirs contain anything beyond the summary file — i.e. every real experiment (executors write CSVs/chunks beside metrics.json).
sources.fix: Same tar-pull engine as above with server-side filtering: `ssh <target> 'cd <path> && find <subdir> -name <pattern> | tar c -T -' | tar x` — the remote find honors the include list, one round trip, transfers only matching files. This is the single most-upstream fix for the whole 'last big unfixed transfer surface': it converts the per-task fallback pull from GB to KB, independent of delta/checkpointing.

sources.name: Trace-ingest pull (_ingest_task_traces) is a SECOND full pull of the same subtree it just pulled
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py:508-516 (call site, fires unconditionally after every successful per-task metrics reduce) and :575-592 (the second rsync_pull of the SAME scoped_subdir, include=[TRACE_TRANSPORT_FILENAME])
sources.mechanism: T4 ingestion-at-harvest issues a separate rsync_pull of the identical scoped results subtree into _per_task_traces, differing only in the include pattern. On rsync hosts this is cheap (filtered + delta). On the Windows scp path (include ignored, no delta) it re-transfers the ENTIRE per-task results tree a second time, back-to-back with the metrics pull that just fetched the same bytes.
sources.cost: Estimate: exactly doubles the per-task-fallback harvest transfer — a second 20-90 min pull per the arithmetic above (same 1.18 GB / ~1 MB/s subtree). The comment 'one extra rsync on the already-flowing per-task seam' (line 546) understates this by ~1000x on the scp path.
sources.trigger: Every no-combiner per-task-fallback harvest on a Windows control plane (the common @register_run SSH-sweep shape, per aggregate_flow.py:696-698). Runs with a combiner skip it.
sources.fix: Fold traces into the ONE pull: pass include=[summary_name, TRACE_TRANSPORT_FILENAME] on the metrics pull at :414 and let _ingest_task_traces read from the already-landed _per_task_results mirror — one round trip instead of two, zero extra bytes. (Falls out for free once server-side filtering exists, but is a one-line batch even today on rsync hosts.)

sources.name: pull_summaries pull is NOT run-scoped — the finding-19 whole-root pull survives on this surface
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py:1651-1658: `sp = rsync_pull(..., remote_subdir=results_subdir, ...)` — raw results_subdir, never routed through _run_scoped_results_subdir (defined :324-353, used only by the fallback/trace pulls at :413 and :576)
sources.mechanism: Finding 19 (run-12) scoped the per-task fallback pull to the run's result_dir_template static prefix precisely because 'pulling the whole results/ root drags every prior run's outputs through the transfer — the scp fallback cannot include-filter — turning a small metrics pull into an 1800s timeout' (:329-333). The pull_summaries branch was not given the same scoping: it pulls the entire shared results/ root, accumulating every prior run's outputs, and on Windows the summary_glob include is ignored on top.
sources.cost: Estimate: identical shape to the measured finding-19 failure — grows with campaign history; a shared results/ root with N prior runs ≈ N × per-run tree. The original finding-19 measurement was an 1800s timeout on exactly this unscoped shape.
sources.trigger: Any aggregate with spec.pull_summaries=true (the min_rows / column-check briefs rely on it) in an experiment dir with more than one prior run sharing results_subdir.
sources.fix: Route through the existing one definition: `remote_subdir=_run_scoped_results_subdir(experiment_dir, run_id, record, results_subdir)` — the same call the two sibling pulls already make. Pure reuse, no new machinery.

sources.name: _combine_missing runs one SSH exec per wave, serially, with serial force-retries
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py:289-321 (per-wave loop × per-attempt loop) → ops/aggregate/combine.py:114-217 → infra/transport/_combiner.py:20-63 (one ssh_run per invocation); combine.py also re-reads sidecar+journal per wave (:146,:152)
sources.mechanism: For W missing waves the loop issues W separate cold SSH connections (native Windows has no working ControlMaster — memory: demo-windows-hoffman2-env), each running `python3 .hpc/_hpc_combiner.py --wave N` on the login node; a failing wave repeats up to combiner_max_retries+1 times with force. The combiner itself could iterate waves in one process — the per-wave split exists only for journal granularity.
sources.cost: Estimate: W × (cold SSH handshake ~2-5s + login-node python start ~1-2s + combine work). A 20-wave run never combined during monitoring = 20+ round-trips ≈ 1.5-3 min of pure connection/interpreter overhead before any combine work; a 100-wave campaign ≈ 5-12 min. Plus HPC_SSH_SAFE_INTERVAL pacing per connection when the ban-driver throttle is on (transport __init__.py:828/1354).
sources.trigger: Every aggregate where monitoring didn't already combine the waves (the skip-monitor / straight-to-aggregate path, and any harvest after a monitor gap). Zero-cost when combined_waves is already complete.
sources.fix: One SSH invocation combining ALL missing waves: extend the deployed combiner with `--waves 3,7,9` (or `--all-missing --run-id X`) emitting a per-wave ok/fail JSON summary; combine_wave journals all outcomes from the one result. N round-trips → 1. (The cluster-final-reduce path below subsumes it further.)

sources.name: Cluster-final reduce (#254) — the 1-RTT, KB-pull path — exists but is opt-in behind an env var; the pull-everything path is the default
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py:1572-1576 (`if os.environ.get("HPC_CLUSTER_FINAL_REDUCE") == "1"`), implementation :882-958 + infra/transport/_combiner.py:109-137
sources.mechanism: The default path pulls every _combiner/wave_*.json partial locally and reduces them (aggregate_flow.py:1577-1591); on Windows scp that is an unfiltered pull of the whole _combiner dir with no delta, re-transferred on every re-aggregate. The already-built alternative runs `--final` on the cluster (one ssh_run) and pulls a single KB metrics_aggregate.json — documented as byte-for-byte identical output (:896-898) — but only fires when the operator remembers to export HPC_CLUSTER_FINAL_REDUCE=1.
sources.cost: Arithmetic: hundreds of wave partials × KB-MB each, re-pulled per aggregate invocation with no delta on the scp path — tens of seconds to minutes per harvest for large campaigns, vs ~5-10s (1 SSH + 1 KB pull) on the built path. Also removes the per-wave incremental-include machinery (:200-234) from the hot path entirely.
sources.trigger: Every default-path SSH aggregate of a multi-wave run; grows with wave count and with re-aggregation frequency (campaign loops re-aggregate per iteration).
sources.fix: Flip the default: use cluster-final reduce whenever the combiner is deployed and the activation resolves, falling back to local reduce on its failure (the fallback chain already exists). The env var becomes the opt-OUT debug knob. This is the standing-ruling shape: eliminate the transfer rather than delta-ing it.

sources.name: min_rows gate runs the cluster status reporter TWICE to compute one diff
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py:237-286 (_nonempty_failing_task_ids: `complete_lenient = _complete_ids(0); complete_strict = _complete_ids(min_rows)`) — two full ssh_status_report round-trips, self-described at :261 ('two SSH round-trips')
sources.mechanism: To find tasks complete-at-0-rows but not-at-N-rows, the reporter is executed twice on the login node, each a cold SSH + login-python start + a full scan of every task's result file. The second invocation re-reads exactly the files the first just read; the diff is two set subtractions that could be done from ONE report if the reporter returned per-task row counts.
sources.cost: Estimate: 2 × (cold SSH ~2-5s + reporter scan over all task files, seconds to tens of seconds for hundreds of tasks on a loaded login node) ≈ 10-60s per aggregate with min_rows>0; half of it pure duplication.
sources.trigger: Every aggregate-flow with spec.min_rows > 0 (the Check-1 non-empty-rows gate).
sources.fix: Have the deployed reporter emit `rows_observed` per task in one pass; compute both complete-sets locally from the single report. One round-trip, one file scan.

sources.name: aggregate-preflight fans out via `hpc-agent` CLI subprocesses — 5+ seconds of interpreter cold-start per sub-call, plus install-commands re-runs on every aggregate
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_preflight.py:63-88 (SubCall argv=["hpc-agent", ...]), :132-144 (reconcile subprocess), ops/_preflight_common.py:62-71 (_run_subprocess → run_capture_bounded); invoked from every aggregate-check via ops/aggregate_blocks.py:354
sources.mechanism: install-commands and load-context each spawn a fresh `hpc-agent` process — full Python interpreter + package import — despite being primitives of the SAME already-imported package; the conditional reconcile is a third subprocess run serially after the fan. install-commands additionally rewrites ~/.claude/{commands,skills,agents} on EVERY aggregate invocation, work that is only needed on version change.
sources.cost: MEASURED: `time hpc-agent capabilities` = 5.36s wall on this box (run just now, this session). So per aggregate-check: ~5.4s for the parallel pair (bounded by the slower cold start) + ~5.4s serial reconcile subprocess when it fires (plus its SSH) ≈ 5-11s of pure process overhead before any real work.
sources.trigger: Every aggregate-check (the hpc-aggregate skill's first block), every campaign iteration that routes through it.
sources.fix: Call the primitives in-process through the registry (they are same-package functions; the subprocess boundary buys no isolation the envelope doesn't already provide), and gate install-commands on a version-stamp check (skip when ~/.claude already carries this wheel's stamp) — eliminates both the cold starts and the redundant reinstall work.

census.notes: Stage D audit, read-only, at main=e2ff358a. Headline: the PULL is architecturally where the push was BEFORE tonight's fixes — findings 1-2 are the mirror-image gap the mission asked about (no delta, no cache, no batching, no checkpointing, and on the live Windows path no include-filtering either, since _scp_pull is the real engine whenever rsync is absent). The single most-upstream move is a tar-based pull engine with server-side find-filtering (finding 2's fix); it collapses findings 1-4 (delta/checkpoint need shrinks ~1000x once only KB-scale summary files transfer). Findings 3 and 4 are independently cheap (S) and worth doing regardless. Also noted but below threshold / not filed: _scp_pull stages via system-temp TemporaryDirectory then shutil.move — a cross-drive move degrades to a full local copy of the pulled bytes (seconds for GB pulls, C:-only setups unaffected); _read_remote_sidecar adds one bounded SSH only when the local sidecar is absent (edge case); combine_wave re-reads sidecar+journal per wave (local, sub-second); harvest-guard's ≤330s breaker-open wait is deliberate half-open-probe design, not avoidable latency. Out-of-scope confirmed excluded: scheduler queue wait, human decision boundaries. Key files: src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_flow.py, src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/__init__.py, src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/_combiner.py, src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate/combine.py, src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_preflight.py, src/hpc_agent/ops/aggregate_blocks.py. The 5.36s CLI cold-start number is measured this session on this box; run-12/13 transfer numbers are cited from the finding annotations embedded in the source (aggregate_flow.py:87-101, :329-333, :396-401, :442) and the mission's own measured context.

Baseline anatomy: every ssh_run (remote.py:714) = breaker file-read + slot O_CREAT claim + optional throttle + subprocess cold ssh dial + remote `timeout -k 10 ... bash -c` wrapper. guarded_call/breaker overhead itself is sub-millisecond in the healthy state (check_circuit = one lock-free JSON read, ssh_circuit.py:268-283; record_connection_success = one read, no write when healthy, :385-401) — NOT a latency source; the latency lives in the dial, the serial exec counts, and the wait constants. End-to-end exec count for a typical S2->canary->S3->watch->S4 run (read from call sites): S2 chain ~6-8 execs (probe true, uv preflight, mkdir prelude, batched tar ship [fixed], test -f executor, mkdir -p logdir, qsub); canary ~15-30 poll execs (3s fast-start ramping to 30s ceiling, verify_canary.py:238-256) + 3-4 post-terminal verification execs; S3 submit 1 (bin path cached, login shell amortized per _remote_base.py:230-242); watch ~40-80 execs for a 3h job (floor 10s, adaptive doubling to 300s cap, monitor_flow.py:140-153); S4 harvest/aggregate ~4-6. Total ~70-130 ssh execs/run. On this box multiplexing is fragile: named-pipe ControlMaster is default-on (ssh_options.py:539-598) but has a known runtime failure mode (getsockname 'Not a socket', :273-307) and a config-forced disable (:426-460), and MEMORY records ControlMaster broken on native Windows as the run-7/8 root cause — so the realistic Windows path is cold dial per exec. Windows has NO select-reader either (remote.py:501 gate), so the backgrounded-child stall class persists there. The single most-upstream fix across findings 1/2/7/9/11 is the same object: flip HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh to default after live validation (the scope-by-constraint trigger explicitly says revisit post-run-13; reaper defect F56/finding-24 is fixed, F55 dispatched-guard is in, keepalives are library-native) — it deletes the handshake per exec, moots the safe_interval throttle (fail2ban counts connection OPENS, which drop from ~100/run to ~1/host), decongests the 2-slot limiter (8 channels ride one slot), and replaces Windows pipe-EOF fragility with library channel framing. Excluded per brief: push hash cache, batched tar ship, pull-mirror excludes, worker crash disclosure (all fixed at HEAD); scheduler queue wait; human think-time.

sources.name: Cold SSH dial per one-shot exec (Windows multiplexing fallback)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/remote.py:834-877 (per-attempt argv + subprocess spawn); src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_options.py:273-307 (named-pipe runtime-broken -> unmultiplexed fallback), :426-460 (config-forced disable), :539-598
sources.mechanism: Each ssh_run spawns a fresh ssh.exe; when named-pipe ControlMaster is runtime-broken (getsockname 'Not a socket'), config-force-disabled, or OpenSSH<8, every exec pays full TCP+kex+auth handshake. A typical S2->canary->S3->watch->S4 run makes ~70-130 ssh execs (counted from call sites: ~6-8 S2 chain, 15-30 canary polls + 4 post-terminal, 1-2 S3, 40-80 watch polls at 10-300s adaptive, 4-6 S4).
sources.cost: ~1-3s per exec (ssh_options.py:546-548 own docstring: 'fresh handshake per call (~1-2 seconds each, hundreds of seconds per submit/monitor/aggregate cycle)'; run-6 broker measured ~150x speedup live per MEMORY). 70-130 execs x 1-3s = ~2-6 min pure handshake per run; worse under Hoffman2 evening MaxStartups load (31s handshake observed, submit_flow.py:156-158).
sources.trigger: Every remote op on native Windows when the named-pipe ControlMaster fallback fires (observed broken on this box's OpenSSH build, run-7/8 root cause) or HPC_NO_SSH_MULTIPLEX/config probe disables multiplexing; POSIX unaffected (Unix-socket ControlPath works).
sources.fix: Make the asyncssh persistent-channel engine the default transport (see next finding) — one handshake per host per process, zero per-exec dial; ControlMaster becomes irrelevant on Windows.

sources.name: asyncssh engine built and hardened but default OFF
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_engine.py:117-121 (ENGINE_ENV, default off), :192-194; consumed at remote.py:800-824
sources.mechanism: The persistent per-host asyncssh engine (breaker-gated, slot-held, keepalive-native, F55 dispatched-guard + F56 drain-not-sever both fixed) only runs under HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh; unset means every capture-mode ssh_run takes the one-shot cold-dial path. The engine eliminates the handshake, the Windows pipe-EOF stall class, and the throttle (engine path returns at remote.py:805 BEFORE throttle_connection at :832), and its 8-session semaphore (_MAX_SESSIONS, ssh_engine.py:148) lets 8 concurrent commands share ONE slot.
sources.cost: The entire finding-1 cost (~2-6 min/run handshake) plus finding-7 slot contention plus finding-9 Windows stalls are all paid only because the default is off. Broker predecessor measured ~150x on the command channel live (run-6, MEMORY).
sources.trigger: Always, unless the operator exports HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh per process (detached workers must inherit it too).
sources.fix: Post-run-13 live validation on a quiet cluster, flip the default to asyncssh with 'native' as the opt-out (the maintainer's own scope-by-constraint trigger names post-run-13 as the revisit point; the reaper defect that justified default-off is fixed). Hard fallback to one-shot already guarantees never-worse-than-today.

sources.name: S2/S3 submit chain: 4-6 serial single-purpose ssh execs
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_flow.py:197 (probe 'true'); src/hpc_agent/infra/runtime_preflight.py:89 (uv probe); src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/__init__.py:1265 (mkdir prelude); src/hpc_agent/infra/backends/_remote_base.py:154 (test -f executor), :297 (mkdir -p log_dir), :261/:273 (submit)
sources.mechanism: The submit stage issues a serial chain of one-command round-trips: reachability probe, uv preflight, mkdir prelude, deploy, executor test -f, log-dir mkdir -p, then the qsub/sbatch exec. The test/mkdir/probe legs are read-only-or-idempotent one-liners that could ride ONE compound command (or piggyback the submit's own bash -lc round trip, exactly as the __HPC_SUBMIT_BIN__ marker already piggybacks binary resolution, _remote_base.py:268-273).
sources.cost: 3-5 collapsible round-trips x (1-3s dial + ~0.3-1s command) ~= 8-20s per submit stage, paid twice (S2 canary submit + S3 main submit). Login-shell leg measured 1.2s server-side, 22.8s outlier (_remote_base.py:232-234, proving-run-2 measurement).
sources.trigger: Every submit-s2 and submit-s3.
sources.fix: Batch the pre-submit probes into one compound sentinel-sectioned command (test -f executor && mkdir -p logdir && command -v uv, echoing per-check markers), and fold it into the deploy or submit round trip; keep the reachability probe only as the first command's implicit success. One round-trip instead of 4-6.

sources.name: Canary post-terminal verification: 3-4 serial ssh execs at the fleet gate
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/verify_canary.py:1039-1052 (fetch_task_logs), :458 (cat _runtime.json), :340 (checkpoint python probe), :1289 (sha probe); src/hpc_agent/infra/cluster_logs.py:102
sources.mechanism: After the canary goes terminal, the gate runs sequential single-purpose execs: stderr-tail fetch (FOUND/MISSING script), _runtime.json cat, optionally the checkpoint round-trip python probe (which also pays remote_activation = conda activate + python startup), and a sha probe. Each is a separate cold dial; all read state that one compound script could emit in sentinel-separated sections.
sources.cost: 3-4 execs x (1-3s dial + 0.5-5s remote, python-under-activation legs at the high end) ~= 8-25s added at the exact human-visible canary->S3 decision boundary. Estimate: 4 x avg 4s = 16s.
sources.trigger: Every canary verification (once per submit pipeline), plus again per verify pass on watch failures.
sources.fix: One compound remote script per verification: tail + cat _runtime.json + checkpoint probe + sha in a single bash -c with section sentinels, parsed client-side; the activation prefix is then paid once instead of per-probe.

sources.name: fetch_task_logs: one ssh exec per (task_id x job_id) probe
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/cluster_logs.py:77-102 (nested for tid / for job_id loop around remote.ssh_run)
sources.mechanism: Log fetching loops task_ids, and per task walks job_ids newest-first, issuing a separate ssh exec per candidate path ('if [ -f p ]; then echo FOUND; tail ...'). A failed run with F failed tasks over J resubmit jobs costs up to F x J round trips; every miss (MISSING) is a full dial that returned one word.
sources.cost: F x J execs x 1-3s. Live shape: 10 failed tasks x 2 jobs = up to 20 dials ~= 20-60s just to collect tails. Canary case is 1x1-2 (small); the S4/failure-triage case is where it bites.
sources.trigger: Any multi-task failure triage, watch anomaly rendering, or resubmit-history run (multiple job_ids in the sidecar).
sources.fix: Emit ONE remote script that loops all candidate paths server-side (for p in ...; do echo MARKER $p; [ -f $p ] && tail -n50 $p; done) and parse the sentinel-framed output — N probes in one round trip, same FOUND/MISSING semantics.

sources.name: Status reporter per-poll re-pays activation + import + full task walk
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/cluster_status.py:159-172 (cmd = cd && activation && python -c 'import hpc_agent' && python -m ...reduce.status); consumed per poll at verify_canary.py:884 and monitor_flow.py (reconcile.py:90/:142)
sources.mechanism: Every watch/canary poll execs a fresh remote python: conda activation prefix, an import-guard python startup, then the reporter module which re-reads the run sidecar + tasks.py and re-walks the results tree from scratch. Nothing is cached across polls and nothing is event-driven, so a 3h watch pays this 40-80 times; the reporter walk scales with task count.
sources.cost: Server-side: activation + 2 python startups ~= 2-6s/poll on a loaded login node (run-12 evidence: the 2700-task walk was wire-silent for many minutes, ssh_options.py:684-690 comment); plus the finding-1 dial. Over a 3h watch: 40-80 polls x 3-8s ~= 2-10 min of remote compute re-done, and slow terminal DETECTION adds up to one full backed-off interval (300s cap, monitor_flow.py:147) after the job actually finishes.
sources.trigger: Every status-watch / canary poll; worst for large task counts and backed-off long jobs.
sources.fix: Event-driven terminal detection: the cluster-side watcher infrastructure already exists and already piggybacks on the same ssh call (watcher_run_dir, cluster_status.py:143-149) — invert it so the remote side writes a terminal/summary file on state change and the poll becomes a cheap cat of that file (no python, no walk), with the full reporter run ONCE at terminal. For fleets, ssh_batch_scheduler_states (cluster_status.py:253) already collapses N runs to one query — route the per-run watch through it between full reports.

sources.name: Per-host slot limiter: 2 slots fleet-wide, whole-call hold, polled wakeup
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_slots.py:107 (DEFAULT_MAX_CONNECTIONS=2), :26-32 (whole-call hold approximation), :112-123 (wait 120s max, poll 1s doubling to 8s), guarded at ssh_circuit.py:450
sources.mechanism: Every ssh/scp/rsync holds one of 2 per-host slots for the WHOLE subprocess call (spawn to exit) — a 60s reporter or a 30-min rsync monopolizes a slot for its full duration. A third concurrent caller (driving agent + detached worker + mcp-serve is a routine trio) polls for a free slot at 1->8s doubling intervals: detection of a freed slot lags up to 8s (polling, not event), and a saturated host wedges callers up to 120s then raises SshSlotWaitTimeout.
sources.cost: MEASURED: F-N (run #10) — a process sat blocked ~15 min acquiring a slot (ssh_slots.py:126-129 cites it). Steady-state: up to 8s wake latency per contended acquire; with a fleet watch + harvest overlapping, tens of seconds per boundary.
sources.trigger: 3+ concurrent ssh-family calls to one host across processes — routine when a detached worker's poll loop overlaps the driver's probes or a long pull.
sources.fix: The engine collapses this structurally: one persistent connection holds ONE slot and multiplexes 8 sessions (ssh_engine.py:51-55, 148), so today's 2-slot cap serves ~16 concurrent commands. Short of that, event-driven wakeup (watch the slot dir / a released-slot touch file) removes the 8s poll lag; do NOT raise the cap (it is the ban guard).

sources.name: Windows blocking capture: backgrounded-child pipe stall to full timeout
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/remote.py:499-507 (_WINDOWS gate: POSIX-only select reader), :632-680 (_capture_windows blocking communicate)
sources.mechanism: The close-pipes-on-exit select reader (#209) that returns the instant the foreground remote process exits is POSIX-only; Windows uses blocking communicate(), so any remote command whose backgrounded child inherited the stdout/stderr pipe stalls the client until SSH_TIMEOUT_SEC even though the command finished. For an unattended poll that turns a finished job into a full-timeout stall per poll.
sources.cost: Up to SSH_TIMEOUT_SEC=60s (remote.py:87) per affected call, vs ~0.2s post-exit latency on POSIX (:507). The module's own docstring names the class: 'a finished job into a full SSH_TIMEOUT_SEC stall' (:485-490).
sources.trigger: Windows client + any remote command that backgrounds a pipe-inheriting child (nohup'd watcher installs, preamble daemons, user executors that daemonize).
sources.fix: Engine default-on eliminates it (asyncssh channels deliver the real exit status independent of stray pipe writers, _to_completed ssh_engine.py:813-828); the Windows-native alternative (overlapped-I/O reader threads with bounded post-exit drain) is strictly more code for the same result.

sources.name: Backoff ladder worst case: ~5.5 min per call against a dead host before the breaker truncates
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/remote.py:325 (_BACKOFF_DELAYS_SEC 2/4/8/16), :344-371 (5 attempts), :87 (60s/attempt); ssh_circuit.py:81 (threshold 3), :84 (300s base cooldown); submit_flow.py:150-152 (probe bound ~11 min)
sources.mechanism: A timing-out host costs 5 attempts x 60s timeout + 30s of sleeps ~= 5.5 min inside ONE ssh_run before returning; the breaker opens at 3 consecutive failures and truncates the tail rungs, but the first ~3 x 60s+sleeps ~= 3.2 min is always paid by the unlucky first caller, and the preflight probe's documented envelope is ~11 min. After the breaker opens, BASE_COOLDOWN_SEC=300s fail-fast holds even if the host healed in seconds (harvest additionally waits <=330s on an open breaker per the e79efd2c deadline-wait).
sources.cost: Fixed constants: 60s x 3-5 attempts + 2/4/8/16s sleeps = 3.2-5.5 min per first-detection; 300s minimum cooldown per breaker trip. Arithmetic from the cited constants.
sources.trigger: Host down/throttled, VPN flap, or a self-inflicted burst tripping the breaker; every subsequent op then eats the cooldown.
sources.fix: Mostly correct-by-design ban protection; the avoidable part is that most breaker trips are SELF-inflicted connection storms (2026-07-05 incident) which the engine's ~1-connection-per-host profile removes, so cooldowns stop firing on healthy clusters. Secondary: ConnectTimeout=15s already fail-fasts unreachable hosts — the 60s ladder rungs only matter for post-connect hangs, so a tighter distinct connect-failure schedule (fail after 2 x 15s, not 3-5 x 60s) would cut dead-host detection ~4x without touching command budgets.

sources.name: Engine idle recycle (120s) undercuts backed-off watch intervals (up to 300s)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_engine.py:135 (IDLE_CLOSE_SEC=120), :143 (sweep 30s); vs src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:147 (adaptive poll cap 300s)
sources.mechanism: Once the engine is on (the finding-2 fix), a long-idle watch that backs off past 120s between polls gets its persistent connection courtesy-recycled by the sweeper between EVERY pair of polls, forcing a breaker-gated reconnect handshake per poll — re-introducing exactly the per-exec dial the engine exists to remove, plus a slot re-acquire, for the steady-state case (a quiet 4h job) the backoff targets.
sources.cost: ~1-3s reconnect per poll for any watch whose interval exceeds 120s; for a 4h idle job at the 300s cap that is ~48 reconnects ~= 1-2.5 min, and each reconnect is a fresh connection OPEN against the host — the very rate fail2ban counts, partially refunding the courtesy the recycle buys.
sources.trigger: HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh + any poll loop whose effective interval > IDLE_CLOSE_SEC (monitor backoff cap 300s; campaign reconcile ticks).
sources.fix: Make the idle-close horizon aware of registered periodic users: a watch loop should declare its cadence (it already computes next_tick_seconds, monitor_flow.py:754) and the engine keep a connection whose next expected use is scheduled — or simply derive IDLE_CLOSE_SEC as max(120, active-loop interval + margin). Keeps the mcp-serve one-shot-verb slot-release fix (the F-B residual) intact.

sources.name: safe_interval throttle serializes every exec when enabled (per-exec, not per-connection)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_throttle.py:38-100; applied per ssh_run at remote.py:832 (one-shot path only; engine path returns at :805 before it)
sources.mechanism: HPC_SSH_SAFE_INTERVAL gates every one-shot ssh SPAWN to >= interval seconds per host. Off by default (0), but it is the sanctioned knob for rate-limiting clusters (Hoffman2 evening MaxStartups — the documented reason it exists), and when set it paces ALL ~70-130 execs of a run even though only connection OPENS matter to fail2ban: with multiplexing broken on Windows every exec IS an open, so the operator who needs it most pays interval x exec-count.
sources.cost: interval x N: at the AiiDA-modelled 30s over a 100-exec run = up to 50 min of pure pacing sleep worst-case (arithmetic; module docstring cites the AiiDA 30s default). Zero when unset (the current default).
sources.trigger: HPC_SSH_SAFE_INTERVAL>0 on a rate-limiting cluster + burst-shaped call patterns (retry storms, parallel probes).
sources.fix: Engine default-on makes the throttle nearly moot (one open per host per process; the engine path already bypasses throttle_connection by construction) — the ban-relevant rate collapses without pacing the work. The module's own tracked follow-up (derive interval from a per-cluster budget in clusters.yaml) is the right residual for the one-shot fallback path.

sources.name: Deploy payload ships _aggregated/ result mirrors back up to the cluster
sources.where: C:\Users\james\.claude\hpc\_detached\submit-s2-causal_tune_linear_fixmask-82ba92e8-a4b00fdb.log lines 4, 7, 83
sources.mechanism: The push exclude list (*.pyc, .git, results, venv, ...) does NOT contain `_aggregated`, so locally-pulled result mirrors (2700 per-task dirs per run, see line 7 `_aggregated/causal_tune_linear-de448128/_combiner`) count as deploy payload: 39,374 files / 9,869.2 MB scanned, and the content-hash delta shipped 18,972 'changed/new' files = 1,181.4 MB of result-derived data upstream for a one-file code fix.
sources.cost: MEASURED: 1,181.4 MB shipped in 621 s (~10.4 min, transport progress lines 84-143); payload scan set 39,374 files/9,869 MB (line 4) inflates both the local hash scan (~23 min, lines 35->82) and the remote manifest phase (~12 min).
sources.trigger: Any submit after an aggregate pull has minted _aggregated/ mirrors in the repo; worst right after a big harvest (run-13 fixmask s2 is exactly this).
sources.fix: Add `_aggregated` (all stack-minted pull-mirror roots) to the deploy default excludes, symmetric with the pull-side exclude fix already landed — result data must never ride the code-deploy path. Eliminates ~1.2 GB transfer and shrinks both hash scans by the mirror subtree.

sources.name: Canary verdict polls a deterministic rc=2 reporter failure for ~30 min
sources.where: a4b00fdb.log line 210 (verify_result.details) + canary2 sidecar submitted_at 2026-07-15T22:42:28Z vs terminal.json ts 23:12:43Z
sources.mechanism: Canary watch polled to its deadline while EVERY poll returned the same deterministic `status reporter failed (rc=2): sidecar_not_found` (plus python 3.11->3.13 module-reload warning). The deterministic-env 3-strike escalation exists but keys on rc 126/127 (seen firing in status-watch logs); rc=2 sidecar_not_found is treated as transient and polled to timeout even after the scheduler shows the job terminal.
sources.cost: MEASURED: 30.25 min submit->verdict (22:42:28Z -> 23:12:43Z); avoidable portion ~20-25 min (canary runtime is ~1 min; queue wait small at 15:42 local).
sources.trigger: Cluster env skew makes the reporter unable to write/read the sidecar — every run-13 canary on carc hit it.
sources.fix: Extend the deterministic-failure classifier: N identical reporter errors (any rc) after the scheduler reports the job terminal => escalate reporter_unreachable immediately instead of polling to deadline.

sources.name: Post-bulk-transfer ssh burst trips the circuit breaker (cold ssh per op)
sources.where: a4b00fdb.log lines 145-161 (circuit OPENED at hb~2919s right after transfer, closed hb~3279s)
sources.mechanism: Immediately after the 621 s tar|ssh transfer, the pipeline issues a burst of fresh cold ssh connections (prune check, canary staging); 3 consecutive connection failures on the throttled/fork-starved login node open the breaker for a fixed 300 s, then one half-open probe. ControlMaster is broken on native Windows so every op is a new TCP+auth handshake counted by the cluster's intrusion filter.
sources.cost: MEASURED: ~360 s breaker dead-wait in a4b00fdb (lines 148->161) + the 3 embedded 60 s failed attempts ~= 9 min; same pattern opens the breaker in submit-s3-...-1af806fe.log.
sources.trigger: Any multi-op sequence on discovery2 right after sustained ssh load; reproduces on both s2 and s3.
sources.fix: One persistent multiplexed channel per host for the whole worker lifetime (in-process ssh library, since OpenSSH ControlMaster is unavailable on win32) — one connection, N commands; the breaker then guards only genuine outages.

sources.name: 60 s remote-command timeout too tight for the conda/module-load preamble; timeouts feed the breaker
sources.where: C:\Users\james\.claude\hpc\_detached\submit-s3-causal_tune_linear_fixmask-82ba92e8-1af806fe.log (attempts 1-3)
sources.mechanism: Every status/reporter command runs `cd && module load conda && source .../conda.sh && ...` under a 60 s timeout. On a loaded login node the env preamble alone exceeds 60 s; each timeout is counted as a connection-level failure, so 3 slow-but-working commands open the 300 s breaker.
sources.cost: MEASURED: s3 log = 60+60+60 s timeouts + 2/4/8 s backoff + 300 s cooldown ~= 490 s before the first successful op; recurs on every op while the login node is loaded.
sources.trigger: discovery2 login node under fork/CPU pressure (evening throttle window per prior runs).
sources.fix: Make the reporter preamble-free: read the sidecar with bare `python3` stdlib or `cat` (no login shell, no module load, no conda) — cuts per-poll remote cost from tens of seconds to <1 s AND stops feeding false connection-failures to the breaker; separately, account command-timeouts distinctly from connect-failures in breaker math.

sources.name: Aggregate re-runs reproduce byte-identical deterministic failures
sources.where: aggregate-run-causal_tune_linear-de448128-971962d9.log vs -a2729099.log (cmp: IDENTICAL, 352,680 B, foreign-rows 2701 vs 2700) and -fea08b1c.log vs -e2a8e5f8.log (identical 'no _combiner' pair)
sources.mechanism: The full aggregate pipeline (including the results pull) was re-executed at 18:09 and again at 19:38 against unchanged inputs, reproducing the exact same integrity error byte-for-byte; then the same pattern repeated at 21:42/22:43 with a different deterministic error. Nothing in the stack detects 'same spec + same tree state => same verdict' and short-circuits.
sources.cost: MEASURED gaps: 89 min between identical attempts 2-3, 61 min between identical attempts 4-5; each re-attempt redoes the pull/scan work (pull alone >=1800 s per the timeout finding). Run-12 aggregate night: ~5.7 h wall for 5 failed attempts, 2 of which were pure repeats.
sources.trigger: Agent/human retries an aggregate whose failure is deterministic (integrity check, missing combiner).
sources.fix: Deterministic-failure memo keyed on (resolved input spec, remote tree fingerprint): identical re-invocation returns the cached verdict as needs-decision instantly; additionally run the cheap foreign-rows count remotely BEFORE the pull so the integrity check costs one round-trip, not a mirror pull.

sources.name: Flat 1800 s scp-pull timeout kills legitimate bulk pulls, non-resumably
sources.where: aggregate-run-causal_tune_linear-de448128-7b207e14.log ('scp pull ... timed out after 1800s'), terminal ts 2026-07-12T00:30:22Z
sources.mechanism: The results pull (full results/causal_tune_linear tree -> _aggregated) runs under a fixed 1800 s deadline with no progress awareness; a large-but-progressing pull is killed at exactly 30 min and restarts from zero on retry (scp, not resumable, no per-batch checkpoint — the push side got batching+checkpointing tonight, the pull side did not).
sources.cost: MEASURED: 1800 s wasted (constant in the error message) + a full re-pull on the next attempt; contributed to the 5.7 h aggregate night.
sources.trigger: Any aggregate whose results tree exceeds ~1800 s of link time (~3.4 GB at the observed 1.9 MB/s) — pre-mirror-excludes pulls hit it routinely.
sources.fix: Mirror the push-side fix on the pull path: batched transfer with per-batch checkpointing and a stall-based (progress-aware) deadline instead of a flat wall-clock constant — a moving transfer is never killed, a stalled one dies in ~60 s.

sources.name: Status-watch relaunched hourly against a deterministic fork-exhausted reporter
sources.where: status-watch-causal_tune_linear-de448128-{9530eabf,e7e46a4d,f00b93f5,ba3c3ce1,13583c6c,3fdb9356,c5ffb7d2}.log (02:16->13:22 Jul 11); brief elapsed_seconds 405.0 and 274.9
sources.mechanism: Each watch burns 3 poll ticks x heavyweight remote command (login shell sourcing .bashrc + module load conda) all failing rc=127 'fork: Resource temporarily unavailable', escalates reporter-UNREACHABLE, attempts a terminal harvest that also fails (sftp 'subsystem request failed on channel 0'), and exits — then gets relaunched ~hourly to reproduce the same verdict for ~11 hours.
sources.cost: MEASURED: 405 s and 275 s per watch (elapsed_seconds in the terminal briefs) x ~9 watches ~= 45-60 min of dead polling, inside an 11 h window where no watch could ever succeed.
sources.trigger: Login-node per-user process limit exhausted (partly self-inflicted by heavyweight per-poll shells) while a run is being monitored.
sources.fix: Two layers: (a) the preamble-free reporter above removes the fork-hungry login shell per poll; (b) persist the reporter-unreachable verdict per (host, env-fingerprint) so a relaunched watch fails fast to a diagnosis brief instead of re-earning the same 3 strikes.

sources.name: Pre-scan remote manifest phase holds ssh for ~12 min on the oversized tree
sources.where: a4b00fdb.log: hb lines 10-33 (child=ssh.exe from 120s to 812s) before '[transport] content-hash scan' at ~852s
sources.mechanism: Before the local hash scan even starts, the worker spends ~700 s in a single ssh.exe child — the remote-side manifest/hash pass over the same 39,374-file deploy set (remote already held 20,402 of them by content-hash, line 83). Scales with total payload file count, i.e. dominated by the _aggregated mirrors that shouldn't be in the payload.
sources.cost: MEASURED: ~700-740 s (hb 120s -> 812s bracket, scan message at ~852s).
sources.trigger: Every s2 push against a large remote tree; additive with the local scan.
sources.fix: Shrinking the payload (the _aggregated exclude) removes most of it; residually, checkpoint the remote manifest cluster-side (the remote analogue of tonight's local .push_hash_cache) so only new/changed remote paths are hashed per push.

sources.name: rsync push retries a deterministic exec failure (/usr/bin/ssh ENOENT) with backoff, across byte-identical worker relaunches
sources.where: submit-s2-causal_tune_linear-de448128-{93c2d381,6bab82d3,63606de8}.log (22:46/22:47/22:49 Jul 10, identical 3,275 B)
sources.mechanism: Git-Bash rsync execs hardcoded /usr/bin/ssh which does not exist on native Windows; the failure is deterministic on this box, yet the transport retried 4-5x with 2/4/8/16 s backoff per worker, and three identical workers were launched within 3 minutes; classified retry-able (`connection unexpectedly closed`).
sources.cost: MEASURED: >=30 s backoff + attempt overhead per worker x >=3 workers, plus the operator loop 22:34->23:53 (9 s2 workers in 80 min) it fed. Mostly historical (rsync since off PATH) but the misclassification persists.
sources.trigger: rsync present on PATH without an explicit -e ssh path on win32.
sources.fix: Pass `-e <native OpenSSH path>` explicitly and classify exec-ENOENT/exit-12 as a non-retryable local config error (retry_safe: false) so it surfaces a remediation instead of a backoff ladder.

census.notes: Timing table (all measured from logs; local = UTC-7). RUN-13 s2 worker a4b00fdb (~21:53->23:12:43Z, ~79 min end-to-end for a FAILED canary): startup+payload warnings ~110 s; remote manifest phase ~700 s; local content-hash scan ~1,385 s (23 min, 39,374 files — the already-fixed .push_hash_cache targets repeat scans; residual first-scan cost is governed by the _aggregated-payload finding); transfer 1,181.4 MB / 621 s; breaker trip+cooldown ~540 s; canary2 submit->reporter_unreachable verdict 1,815 s. RUN-13 s3 1af806fe: >=490 s dead on 60 s timeouts + 300 s breaker before any work. RUN-12 aggregate night (Jul 11): 5 attempts 17:30->22:43 local ~5.7 h — 1800 s pull timeout, then two byte-identical foreign-rows failures 89 min apart, then two identical no-_combiner failures 61 min apart. RUN-12 s2 storm (Jul 10 22:34->23:53): 9 workers; rsync ENOENT ladder x3 identical workers; the final 23:53 attempt crashed AFTER the canary ran at submit_and_verify.py:115 `KeyError: 'estimator'` in _pull_canary_task0_metrics template.format — a crash-after-an-hour-of-work forcing a full s2 redo (code bug; flagging for cross-check against post-Jul-10 fixes, not counted as a standing latency source). Excluded per scope: the two instant DNS pre-flight failures 21:31/21:38 Jul 15 (discovery1/2 unresolvable — failed fast, correct behavior); cluster queue wait. Fixed constants observed: ssh command timeout 60 s, breaker threshold 3 / cooldown 300 s / backoff 2-4-8-16 s, scp pull deadline 1800 s, hb 30 s. Biggest single lever by measured minutes: keep _aggregated out of the deploy payload (removes ~10 min transfer + most of the 23-min + 12-min scan phases); second: preamble-free reporter (kills the rc=127/60s-timeout/breaker family that cost ~9 min per worker and ~an hour of dead watches).

sources.name: Adaptive-backoff tail on time-to-notice-terminal (up to 300s)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:147 (_MAX_ADAPTIVE_POLL_SECONDS=300), :153 (_UNCHANGED_POLLS_BEFORE_BACKOFF=2), :1280-1302 (doubling + sleep)
sources.mechanism: The poll loop doubles effective_interval after 2 unchanged polls, capped at HPC_STATUS_POLL_MAX_SEC=300. A long-quiet job is exactly the one whose terminal event lands mid-interval, so the terminal marker sits unread for up to a full backed-off interval before the census/walk tick sees it; only then do mark_terminal + harvest + the worker exit (the harness rendezvous) fire.
sources.cost: Worst case 300s, expected ~150s at cap, added to every quiet run's terminal notice. Constants measured from code: floor HPC_STATUS_POLL_INTERVAL_SEC=10 (monitor_flow.py:140), spec default 60 (_wire/workflows/monitor_flow.py:31-33), cap 300 (monitor_flow.py:147). Ramp 60→120→240→300 within ~10 min of quiet per the comment at :150-153.
sources.trigger: Any watched run idle (status fingerprint unchanged) for >=2 polls before its last tasks finish — the common shape for hour-scale jobs.
sources.fix: Event-driven terminal notice: replace the client-side sleep with a remote long-poll — ONE bounded ssh exec that waits server-side on the announce dir (until-loop/inotifywait on the marker count, deadline == the current effective interval, ack-gated like read_announcements). Connection count per hour is unchanged or lower, but a terminal marker wakes the client within ~1s of the dispatcher writing it. The adaptive backoff then governs only the exec's server-side deadline, not staleness.

sources.name: Census leg pays TWO ssh execs per tick when wave-combining is on
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:665-668 (per-tick _census_complete_task_ids) + :212-255 (its own ssh_run) vs ops/monitor/announce.py:55-116 (read_announcements, the counts exec)
sources.mechanism: Every census tick issues read_announcements (exec #1: cd + two `ls|wc -l` counts) and then, whenever auto_combine_waves(default True) + wave_map, a SECOND full ssh exec listing the complete-marker filenames for the F28 waves block — even on ticks where the census counts did not move. The listing is a strict superset of the counts: one exec could serve both.
sources.cost: One extra cold ssh dial per tick. Engine is opt-in (remote.py:786-803, HPC_SSH_ENGINE unset by default) and ControlMaster is unreliable on the native-Windows client, so each dial ~1-3s healthy (memory: hoffman2 ~1s morning, 60s+ under evening MaxStartups throttle) plus one of the 2 per-host slots (ssh_slots.py:107). A 4h watch at the 60s floor ≈ 240 avoidable dials; at 2 slots fleet-wide this halves the fleet's effective poll bandwidth.
sources.trigger: Announce-era run with a wave_map sidecar and auto_combine_waves=True (the default) — i.e. the normal campaign shape.
sources.fix: Fold to ONE exec: have read_announcements return the marker FILENAMES (one ack-gated `ls`) and derive complete/failed counts AND the complete task-id set client-side; delete _census_complete_task_ids. Strictly more information for one round-trip instead of two.

sources.name: Pre-announce fallback runs the full reporter walk every tick until the FIRST task finishes
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:678-713 (walk fallback), :309-311 (announce dir 'created lazily on the FIRST marker'), ops/monitor/status.py:174-189 (record_status → ssh_status_report), ops/monitor/announce.py:10-12 (measured cost citation)
sources.mechanism: The census leg engages only once the dispatcher writes its first terminal marker (the announce dir is created lazily). Until then — the whole queue-wait plus the first task's compute, potentially hours — every monitor tick falls back to record_status, the cluster-side per-task reporter walk. On large runs one walk exceeds the poll interval, so the watch degrades to back-to-back walks.
sources.cost: MEASURED: 20-25 min per walk on the run-12 fleet (cited in announce.py:10-12 and reconcile.py:768 — 'a 20-25 min silent status-reporter walk was severed mid-flight'). For a 300-task fleet with an hour of queue wait, that is potentially 2-3 consecutive 20-min walks producing zero information beyond 'nothing finished yet'.
sources.trigger: Every SSH-backend run between submit and its first task's terminal marker; also any pre-announce-wheel run.
sources.fix: Dispatcher creates the announce dir (and optionally per-task .started markers) EAGERLY at run start, so the ONE-readdir census leg owns the lifecycle from tick 1; the F17 scheduler-liveness probe already covers the 'nothing announced, is anything alive?' question without a walk. The walk fallback then decays to genuinely legacy runs only.

sources.name: reconcile runs the 3-way probe (incl. the reporter walk) even when a PARTIAL announcement already answers the lifecycle question
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor/reconcile.py:784-828 — partial announce demoted to progress evidence at :796-803, then the ThreadPool probe with fut_status=_ssh_status_report at :804-817
sources.mechanism: A full announcement settles with no probe (good, :793-795), but a partial one (announced>0, <total) is threaded in as evidence only and the heavy 3-way probe still runs — including the per-task reporter walk — although announce counts + the alive-jobs check (cheap, already probed in parallel) suffice for the mid-flight verdict 'still in flight, N complete'.
sources.cost: One reporter walk per reconcile invocation on a mid-flight announce-era run: minutes on large runs (same 20-25 min measured class as above; the walk dominates the probe since waves+alive are single bounded execs). status-snapshot --reconcile and campaign reconcile ticks pay it.
sources.trigger: reconcile-journal / status-snapshot(reconcile=True) / campaign reconcile tick on an announce-era run that is mid-flight (partial census).
sources.fix: When any announcement is present, drop fut_status from the probe: lifecycle = census counts + alive probe + wave listing; run the walk only on the settle path when per-task detail (failure_features, min_rows demotion) is actually needed for a terminal verdict.

sources.name: Fleet monitoring is N independent per-run pollers — no cross-run census batching, contending for 2 SSH slots
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/status_blocks.py:619-648 (one detached watcher per run_id), monitor_flow census is per-run (.hpc/announce/<run_id>), infra/ssh_slots.py:107 (DEFAULT_MAX_CONNECTIONS=2), :112-118 (slot wait 1-8s poll, 120s cap); contrast ops/monitor/batch_status.py (one scheduler query per login node — exists but unused by monitor_flow)
sources.mechanism: Each in-flight run gets its own detached status-watch worker running its own monitor_flow loop; each tick issues 1-2 per-run ssh execs against the same login node. N runs = N (or 2N, finding above) cold dials per interval, serialized through the fleet-wide 2-slot limiter (waiters poll 1-8s, give up at 120s → treated as a transient fault → an EXTRA effective_interval sleep, monitor_flow.py:786-858). The batching pattern already exists for scheduler state (batch_status) but not for the announce census.
sources.cost: Estimate: fleet of 10 runs at the 60s floor = 10-20 dials/min to one host through 2 slots; at ~1-3s/dial the slots are ~25-100% utilized, so tail ticks queue seconds-to-tens-of-seconds, and any burst risks the evening MaxStartups throttle (the measured run #7/8 stall root cause: cold-SSH-per-op vs hoffman2 throttle). A slot-wait timeout costs 120s wait + one full backed-off interval (up to 300s) = up to ~7 min for one tick.
sources.trigger: More than ~3-4 concurrently watched runs sharing a login node (a normal campaign fleet).
sources.fix: One fleet-level census per login node per tick: a single ssh exec listing <remote_path>/.hpc/announce/*/ (or a tiny remote script emitting per-run per-state counts) fanned back into every run's journal — the batch_status pattern applied to the announce census. Per-run watchers consume the shared snapshot (last_status.json mtime already supports cheap local reads, ops/monitor/status.py:212-228) instead of each dialing.

sources.name: Serial per-wave combine_wave execs on a tick with a wave burst
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:906-954 — for wave in newly_done: combine_wave(...) one ssh exec each, sequential
sources.mechanism: Waves that complete together (common at fleet end, and after a long backed-off gap several waves land in one diff) are combined one-by-one: each combine_wave is its own cold ssh dial + remote combine. The tick — and therefore terminal detection and the watchdog stamp — is delayed by the whole serial chain.
sources.cost: Estimate: per wave ~1-3s dial + remote combine (seconds, data-dependent); 10 waves landing together ≈ 30-60s+ added to one tick, all through the 2-slot limiter. Arithmetic: 10 × (2s dial + 2-4s combine) ≈ 40-60s.
sources.trigger: auto_combine_waves=True and >=2 waves newly complete on the same tick (bursts amplified by the adaptive backoff's longer diff windows).
sources.fix: Most upstream: move wave-combine cluster-side — the dispatcher already knows when a wave's last task announces; combine there and announce the combined artifact, eliminating client-driven combines entirely. Interim batch: extend combine_wave to accept a wave LIST and run all newly-complete combines in one remote exec.

sources.name: Transient-fault ticks sleep the BACKED-OFF interval instead of the floor
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/ops/monitor_flow.py:781-785 (poll_error → _sleep(effective_interval)) and :843-857 (ssh fault → max(effective_interval, circuit_wait))
sources.mechanism: After a failed poll the loop sleeps effective_interval — which may be backed off to 300s from PRE-fault quiet. But a failed poll yields NO fresh fingerprint: the 'unchanged' inference that justified the backoff doesn't apply, and the run may have gone terminal just before the blip. The breaker-cooldown floor for SshCircuitOpen is correct; using the backed-off interval as the floor for a plain RemoteCommandFailed/OSError blip is not.
sources.cost: Up to 300s of extra staleness per transient blip while backed off (constants: cap 300s, monitor_flow.py:147). A couple of blips near terminal = minutes of added time-to-notice.
sources.trigger: Any transient poll failure (reporter rc!=0, OSError, SshUnreachable/SlotWaitTimeout) occurring while the adaptive interval is above the floor.
sources.fix: On a transient fault, sleep the FLOOR interval (poll_interval_seconds) and reset the backoff state (unchanged_count/last_fingerprint), reserving longer waits for the breaker-cooldown case which already carries its own deadline.

sources.name: poll_until_terminal defaults to a 30s poll on a LOCAL journal file
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/state/journal_poll.py:86 (poll_interval_seconds=30.0 default), :108-114 (sleep loop); referenced as the sanctioned orchestrator wait from _kernel/lifecycle/detached.py:140
sources.mechanism: The orchestrator-side rendezvous helper re-reads the local run record every 30s. The read costs milliseconds (local JSON), so the interval buys nothing; the detached runner's journal write goes unnoticed for up to 30s. The pid-based sibling (wait-detached, 2s pid probe, _wire/queries/wait_detached.py:39) already shows the cheap-local cadence.
sources.cost: Up to 30s dead air per rendezvous, ~15s expected — per block boundary that routes through it. Defensible arithmetic: local load_run ≈ 1-5ms, so even a 2s interval is <0.25% duty cycle.
sources.trigger: Any caller using poll_until_terminal at defaults after arming a detached runner.
sources.fix: Event-driven: wait on the worker's lease pid (wait-detached) and read the journal once on exit — the write happens-before the exit by construction; failing that, drop the local default to 2s to match wait-detached (a local stat costs nothing).

census.notes: Scope confirmations requested by the brief: (1) The announce done-marker census IS one bounded ssh exec per tick (read_announcements: cd + ack + two ls|wc -l, announce.py:81-90) — but becomes TWO execs per tick when wave-combining (finding 2). (2) Announce-absent fallback = the full cluster-side reporter walk via record_status, measured 20-25 min on large runs (run-12 findings 20/24, cited in-code) — findings 3 and 4 cover its two firing sites. (3) reconcile-stale and batch-status are already properly batched (one scheduler query per login node) — no findings there; they are the pattern finding 5 asks to extend to the announce census. (4) status-watch detach rendezvous is already event-driven end-to-end on the sanctioned path: child records the block terminal BEFORE exit (status_blocks.py:688-692), harness wakes the agent via backgrounded wait-detached (2s local pid probe — sub-threshold), and the next block-drive tick replays the recorded terminal with ZERO ssh (_replay_watch_terminal). The dominant time-to-notice-terminal cost is therefore inside the worker's own poll cadence (finding 1), not the rendezvous. Run-13 finding 5 (hand-rolled bash watch loop) is a conduct bypass of this sanctioned path, not a code gap — excluded per instructions. (5) Doctor scans (scan_dead_detached_workers, stalled-run scan, open-circuit lines) are pure local filesystem reads — no avoidable latency found. (6) The [hb] heartbeat is a ~30s stderr append from a daemon thread — disclosure only, adds no wall clock. (7) ssh_throttle safe_interval is OFF by default (0.0) — not a live cost; the live pacing constraints are the 2-slot per-host limiter and the 10s poll floor, both folded into findings 1/2/5. (8) Harvest-guard's single breaker-cooldown wait+retry (harvest_guard.py:191-202) is the sanctioned half-open probe on a fault path — judged correct, not avoidable. Cross-stage note: findings 1+2+5 compound — a backed-off fleet whose members each dial twice per tick through 2 slots is the worst case; fixing finding 5 (fleet census) largely subsumes finding 2.

sources.name: Full registry walk on every verb call whenever the fast path is disqualified (CLI-shaping plugin installed, unmapped/grouped verbs, --version/--help)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/cli/dispatch.py:122-216 (_fast_dispatch_enabled/_try_fast_dispatch), src/hpc_agent/cli/_verb_module_map.py
sources.mechanism: _fast_dispatch_enabled returns False when ANY installed plugin exposes register_cli (dispatch.py:163-166) — then EVERY verb, core included, takes register_primitives() + build_parser() (the ~100-module import walk). Also any verb absent from VERB_MODULE_MAP (e.g. install-commands, grouped verbs, mcp-serve/run) always takes the full walk. Additionally load_plugins()'s entry_points() metadata scan runs on every invocation even with zero plugins.
sources.cost: MEASURED on this box (warm): full path 7.2s per invocation vs fast path 1.3-1.8s ('time python -m hpc_agent.cli.dispatch --version' = 7.18s; 'read-decisions --help' = 1.28s; 'submit-s1 --help' = 1.81s). load_plugins() alone MEASURED 0.35s per process with zero plugins installed. The demo env carries hpc-agent-notebook-render (adds a render subcommand → almost certainly register_cli), so in the LIVE env every one of the block-drive loop's many verb subprocesses likely pays ~7s instead of ~1.5s — for a 10-verb-call run that is ~55s of pure parser-rebuild wall clock.
sources.trigger: Every CLI/MCP verb subprocess in an env with a register_cli plugin installed (the live demo/hook envs); every call of an unmapped verb (install-commands rides every submit-preflight); 0.35s entry-point scan unconditionally per process.
sources.fix: Per-verb granularity instead of the wholesale disable: extend the plugin MANIFEST to declare which core verbs (if any) the plugin reshapes, so core verbs keep the fast path when the plugin only ADDS subcommands; add install-commands and the other Tier-3/grouped verbs to the map or give them their own single-verb parsers; cache the plugins-present verdict (a marker keyed on the installed-distribution set) so the 0.35s entry_points() scan is not re-paid by every subprocess.

sources.name: Per-verb-call Python process spawn: block-drive spans and submit-preflight subcalls each pay the full interpreter+import floor
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/_kernel/lifecycle/block_drive.py:321-392 (_block_verb_argv/_run_block_verb), src/hpc_agent/ops/submit_preflight.py:180-253 (SubCall argv=['hpc-agent',...])
sources.mechanism: block-drive chains spans by spawning 'hpc-agent <verb> --spec ...' subprocesses (one per chained span), and submit-preflight fans out FOUR more 'hpc-agent' subprocesses (install-commands, load-context, preflight, resolve-resources). Every child re-pays interpreter start + package import + plugin scan, despite being the same installed package as the parent (meta.func is directly callable in-process).
sources.cost: MEASURED floor: bare 'import hpc_agent' = 1.13s; fast-path verb = 1.3-1.8s; full-path verb = 7.2s. A block-drive tick that chains 2 spans + an S1 that runs submit-preflight = ~6 child processes ≈ 8-12s of pure startup at fast-path rates, ~30-45s if the plugin disables the fast path. Multiplied across the run's many ticks (S1 pre-resolve, S1 resolve, S2, poll cycles, S3, S4, append-decision).
sources.trigger: Every block-drive tick and every submit-preflight invocation, unconditionally.
sources.fix: In-process dispatch for same-package children: have _run_block_verb and submit-preflight's SubCall runner invoke the registered primitive (meta.func) directly — keeping the subprocess path as the seam for deadline-kill/isolation where genuinely needed (the bounded-capture rationale applies only to watch-class verbs; the short verbs need no process boundary). This eliminates N-1 interpreter starts per tick.

sources.name: Uncompressed tar|ssh stream on the live (rsync-less Windows) push path over a slow VPN
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/__init__.py:421-423 (tar c, no -z/--zstd), src/hpc_agent/infra/ssh_options.py:726-806 (Compression pinned 'no' by default)
sources.mechanism: The delta/full tar fallback pipes 'tar c' (no compression flag) into ssh whose Compression option is explicitly pinned 'no' (rationale comment: 'on a fast link compression only adds CPU' — but the live link is a ~2 MB/s VPN). The rsync path uses -az (compressed); the tar path — the ONLY path on native Windows — ships raw bytes.
sources.cost: MEASURED: the live run-13 push shipped 1181 MB at ~2 MB/s ≈ 590s (~10 min); attempt-1 + full retry paid it twice pre-batching. Python source/JSON/CSV payloads typically compress 2-4x with zstd/gzip, so ~5-7 min of the ~10 min was avoidable on that push; every future GB-scale delta pays proportionally. HPC_SSH_COMPRESSION=yes exists but is a hidden env knob nobody sets.
sources.trigger: Any tar-fallback push (native Windows = the live path) with compressible payload over a bandwidth-bound link; worst on first deploys and large deltas.
sources.fix: Compress the tar stream by default on the fallback path: 'tar cz' (or --zstd where available, with a plain-tar fallback probe) or flip HPC_SSH_COMPRESSION default to 'yes' for the tar|ssh legs — payload-aware if desired (skip when the delta is dominated by already-compressed extensions). The remote extract gains 'tar xz' symmetrically. Keep the fast-LAN opt-out as the env knob instead.

sources.name: Remote side re-sha256s the ENTIRE pushable tree on every delta push (no remote quick-check cache)
sources.where: src/hpc_agent/infra/transport/_delta.py:50-101 (_REMOTE_MANIFEST_SNIPPET), _delta.py:323-358 (_remote_push_manifest)
sources.mechanism: Every delete=True push ships the stdlib snippet that os.walks the remote tree and streams-sha256s EVERY file to build the remote manifest — even when nothing changed since the last push. There is no remote (size,mtime)->sha cache; the local side got exactly this cache in run-13 finding 6, the remote side did not.
sources.cost: Estimate from the measured local twin: the same 39,374-file / 9.9 GB set took ~37 min to cold-hash locally (_delta.py:109 comment, measured live). A login node reads Lustre/NFS at ~100-500 MB/s and pays ~1-5 ms/stat on 39k files → ~60-300s of remote CPU+IO per push, every push, inside the bounded-ssh call (and it competes with the login node's throttle budget). For the common 'tree unchanged, ship 0 bytes' re-push this is nearly the whole wall clock.
sources.trigger: Every delete=True rsync-less push (the live Windows path) against a deployed tree with the delta runtime; scales with remote file count and bytes.
sources.fix: Persist the full content manifest (path, size, mtime_ns, sha256) remote-side at push time — extend .hpc/.push_manifest.json, which the push already writes — and have the snippet re-hash only files whose (size, mtime_ns) moved since that manifest, exactly mirroring the local finding-6 cache. A first push / absent manifest falls back to the full hash as today.
